No Poverty.reason: The course analyzes economic systems like 'Capitalism & Culture' and 'Materialism' (Marx), which are foundational to understanding poverty and pro-poor policies (Target 1.b). This provides a strong inferred link.

Zero Hunger.reason: The course includes 'Condorcet-Malthus', whose theories on population and resource limits have a minimal, inferred link to food security and hunger.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: A very tenuous link exists through the study of Freud ('Magic & Mind'), which could be broadly connected to mental health and well-being (Target 3.4).

Quality Education.reason: As a university course, it directly contributes to tertiary education (Target 4.3). It also aims to sharpen academic skills and foster understanding of one's place in time, aligning with sustainable development knowledge (Target 4.7).
evidence: sharpen your academic skills as a presenter, a discussant, and a writer
understand time and our place within it

Gender Equality.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence related to gender equality or women's empowerment was found in the course syllabus.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to clean water, sanitation, or hygiene.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to energy access, renewable energy, or energy efficiency.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course directly analyzes 'Capitalism & Culture' and 'Ideology & Materialism' (Hegel-Marx), which are core theories for understanding economic growth, productivity, and employment structures (Targets 8.2, 8.3).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: Discussions on 'Capitalism & Culture' and 'Modernity & Postmodernity' provide an inferred link to industrialization, technological development, and infrastructure (Target 9.2).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course examines 'Materialism' (Marx) and 'Liberal Democracy', which are central to debates on economic and political inclusion and equality (Targets 10.2, 10.3).

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to urban development, housing, transport, or cultural heritage.

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The course lacks explicit focus on responsible consumption or production patterns, waste management, or sustainable resource use. Any link would be too tenuous.

Climate Action.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to climate change, its impacts, or mitigation/adaptation strategies.

Life Below Water.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or sustainable fisheries.

Life on Land.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to terrestrial ecosystems, biodiversity, deforestation, or land degradation.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: This is a core theme, as the course explicitly explores 'why people have been willing to kill and die for the ways they have believed the future needs to be' and analyzes 'Fascism & Totalitarianism' and 'Liberal Democracy', directly addressing peace, justice, and governance.
evidence: why people have been willing to kill and die

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: No evidence found in the course syllabus that relates to international cooperation, global partnerships, or means of implementation for sustainable development.
